title: Self-harm: understanding and getting help
citation: Hawton, K., et al. (2012). Self-harm in Oxford, England: Characteristics and trends in presentations to hospital, 1996-2010. British Journal of Psychiatry, 201(6), 488-495.
content:
Self-harm refers to any behaviour in which a person deliberately hurts their own body, usually as a way of coping with emotions that feel overwhelming or impossible to express in other ways. It is not the same as a wish to die, though the two can sometimes co-occur and it is always important to understand what someone is experiencing rather than assume.

People self-harm for many reasons. It can provide a temporary sense of release or control when emotions feel out of control. It can be a way of expressing pain that cannot be put into words. It can be a way of feeling something when a person feels emotionally numb. Understanding self-harm as a coping mechanism, one that causes harm but serves a psychological function, is important because it shapes how we respond. Shame and judgement are not helpful. Curiosity and care are.

Self-harm is not attention-seeking, a phrase that dismisses a genuine cry for help even when it is accurate. If someone is communicating distress through their behaviour, that distress is real and deserves a compassionate response.

If you or someone you know self-harms, speaking to a healthcare professional is an important step. GPs, mental health services, and crisis lines can all provide support. There are also therapies that have been shown to help people develop alternative ways of managing distress over time.

Recovery is not linear and there is no single path, but many people who have self-harmed do find that with the right support they are able to build a life in which they no longer need to hurt themselves. That outcome is possible, and getting help is where it begins.
